review_text: The infrastrucure behind Waze is great in theory.  In practice, it breaks down and you can't rely on it.  Local routing is terrible.  It's supposed to remember your favorite routes.  Good Luck!  It keeps putting me back on its esoteric routes rather than adopting my preferred.  You're at an intersection.  Poor GPS location, rather than realizing that all you need to do is turn left to get on your main route home, thinks you're beyond the intersection and routes you straight ahead through a maze of neighborhood byways to get you back on track.  It doesn't know U-turns are legal in Texas (that's the most you'd need to correct going beyond an intersection).  Rather than stick to main roads, it attempts to save a minute or two by routing you through all sorts of neighborhood back alleys, with speed bumps and potholes, etc.  Stop at a light, you get a blizzard of notifications assaulting you, most of which are located miles away and have nothing to do with your route.  Seems to eat your battery like crazy, even more than LTE by itself, if you have your screen set to auto brightness, you'll definitely need a car charger to go more than a few miles (turn screen off, just use voice).

review_text: A curious app that doesn't work that well where I am (Sydney, Australia).  The app relies on users to compile roadmaps both by driving around roads and then revising them online Wiki-style.  The app delivers a sat-nav service, but it's only as good as the quality of the maps, and around Sydney they are full of errors and massive holes which severely compromises the app's usefulness.  Users can also report features like traffic snarls and police speed traps, which are then communicated to other users and which impact on the routing, but use of most of these reporting features by the driver is illegal in NSW so unless your passenger does the updating this is not much use if you want to stay on the right side of the law.  A feature of this app is that it "learns" your favourite routes and then will offer these as navigation options even if they are not optimal - but why would you need to use satnav on a route you knew well?  You need an active data connection at all times to use this app, it doesn't work offline.  Waze doesn't seem to offer any useful features that aren't already available on my phone for free - and the maps and routing are far more accurate in my area.

review_text: Whether your car has a built-in GPS or not, whether you have a standalone GPS with "traffic" or not, whether you have Navigon (etc) with traffic or not - all these don't matter. Waze has very aggressive routing algorithms that actually get you to the destination faster based on the information from other wazers. I'm amazed how well it can re-route when there is an accident that blocks the road or how it ignores streets where it does not know real traffic. Maps are updated monthly and they are user editable so if your neighbourhood has a new streets or intersection, you just visit Waze Editor add a new road and you are done, Waze will start using this new road as soon as new tiles are generated by the server. Fast, easy and fun. Plus it is the most gratifying experience when someone thanks you for the report you've made during the drive about some hidden cop sitting behind the bridge overpass.

review_text: I love messing around with Waze, but that's about all I can do.  It's routing continues to be a mystery (sending me 50+ miles out of my way on a 55 mile drive, for one example), the past week or so it cannot find a GPS signal before I get where I'm going.  It will provide a notice to say there is heavy traffic on some tiny residential street because someone is using Waze and has stopped and forgotten to exit the program, yet when I'm stuck in bumper to bumper traffic there is nothing.  Any time I turn it on it asks if I am going to work or home and if I don't specifically say no it automatically starts the routing function (incorrectly, it has yet to learn the route I drive to work every. single. day. and would rather I detour down congested streets full of stop signs instead of on the main roads I take).  If I really need to find an address or route I use google.  When I'm driving around town and don't need any help with directions I leave Waze on and watch for accidents or police locations.   I hope things get better, I've sent feedback to the developers on these issues.  If this were a twitter or facebook app I would have deleted it long ago.

It also crashes constantly or gets booted from RAM because it's too big or something. review_text: I loved the idea of a social gps, and really wanted to like this app.  But it's really only useful if you only want to use it to see alerts entered by other drivers.  If you actually want to use it to get directions, you are going to find yourself driving 10-20% longer/farther than necessary, at best, or just completely screwed at worst.  There are roads in my neighborhood that have been there for a decade that aren't on the maps.  I've tried fixing them for almost two months, and they still only show on the web map, but not in the app.  There's a bridge that was torn down years ago that Waze believes to still be there, and insists I should use to get home.  I can't fix that either.  The data for several major roads in the area is glitched, and tries to send me through miles of neighborhoods rather than just stay on the major road from one end to the other.  When roads are added in the web site, they default to being one way streets, which has made the maps for this area (Hampton Roads) quite the mess.  I would be surprised if the same problem doesn't exist in other metro areas as well.  Finally, if you try to use this to go anywhere rural, you're even more hosed.

This was one of the first apps I downloaded back onto my new device.  There are a few other social based GPS apps but none are quite as informative and simple to use.  Getting points/achievements for just driving was a neat trick but I was sold on this app when a freak accident broke out on the NJ turnpike resulting in a 2 hour parking lot.  I was not only able to get off just prior to the  traffic buildup, but got home hours before a few friends who got stuck in the traffic before me.  With the snapshot feature and commentary from another wazer, I was able to find out just what caused the horrible backup well before it aired on any of the traffic radio stations.  Waze for the win!  I also like the commitment that staff puts into Waze,  continuing to improve upon the service on a regular basis.

It also never 'learned' my morning route. It still wants to take me several blocks out of my way despite the fact that I go the same route, albeit not the same as the suggested route, every morning. Waze' route may be shorter, but my route is quicker.  I will give Waze another try with the next update. Till then it is unusable.

review_text: I love the map layout and auto-zoom. It adjusts according to speed so the detail is always just right.  I was very surprised at the number of people in my area using Waze. I love to sit at a stop light and watch all the pop-ups showing all the traffic issues around me.  But it shines in a traffic problem.  When I started using it I was going home from work and it took a different route.  It was a new program so I ignored it.  I got 1 mile down the road and traffic was stopped. Normally I would have sat there wondering but this time I had all the reports from other users who were in every part of the traffic jam.  There were Waze users at the cause, on the alternate routes, and I had a running commentary on all possible alternate routes.  I turned around and followed the Waze route and was soon home.  I use it all the time, not always for the maps, but for the ongoing traffic reports.  My only complaint is that it seems to take a long time to learn favorite routes.  I use it every day to go to work for all most a month and even though I tell it not to use highways and take the shortest route and to auto-learn routes  it still wants me to take the highway which is a longer route.

review_text: I used to use Waze on my commute, but starting several weeks ago routing engine started going nuts. When I start navigating back to home, it directs me to a route which clearly goes around extra 4~5 miles when it's completely unnecessary. My destination is straight ahead in less than 1 mile, but it wants me to take a right and literally circle around my neighbor for about 5 miles and come back down to my destination. It was 11pm, so there was no traffic at all, and even if there was traffic, I think going around in circle for 5 miles will take longer than going straight to destination in about 800ft. I know that routing is done on server side and not in the app itself, so this might be the server problem. Anyway it was quite frustrating and currently I'm using Google map instead which works perfectly fine. I hope this is fixed soon. Routing engine is the most important part in navigation and with Waze's purpose, routing has to be efficient and reliable. It's not enough "just to get there" for an app like Waze, which its purpose is to get you there fast and efficiently.

review_text: Quirks aside, I am just glad to find a free GPS with SD support that won't fill up my storage. (I'm looking at you, telenav). It's not perfect but it works well enough that I use it regularly.   The alerts(traffic etc) are annoying and poorly implemented. It doesn't give reports for your route; rather, it gives you reports in a radius around your current location. For instance, it'll often give me a traffic alert for a location 20 miles south of me when it knows perfectly well that I'm driving north. That doesn't help at all. I'd much rather know if there's a traffic jam or speed trap ON MY ROUTE or at least near the general direction I'm heading! It ends up being useless as a traffic beater because of that flaw, and also because it relies entirely on users to provide it with info. The traffic and police alerts are pretty much always inaccurate or at least out of date by several minutes. I'd prefer to have a GPS that syncs with an actual traffic report (like Telenav) but sadly I am stuck with this because it is the only one with SD support.

review_text: I have used this application for about 3 months and must say that while I like some of it's features (traffic, police, etc), it lacks in navigation.  Many times I have entered in a destination only to realize that the navigation is taking me to the incorrect side of the highway where I must make a u-turn (sometimes up to a mile out of my way) to get to where I want to go. I do not have this issue with google maps (navigator), as it will find the route correctly.  Another thing I find annoying is that once a route is in place, it seems that any deviation from that route will still try to route you BACK to the original route.  This may seem like a good idea, but in one case, I was in the express lanes, it routed me the non-express lanes, and then wanted me to go back to the express lanes.  Not too much of an issue, but I was not able to get back onto the express lanes so I continued in the non-express lanes.  It routed me off the highway at the next exit and then back onto the original highway (express) and wasted about 10 minutes, 3 miles and a toll to get back onto the express lanes when everything should have been fine in the local.  I'll stick to better routing and updated maps.

review_text: I discovered Waze several months ago when I went to buy a new GPS and a commenter shared how much they loved Waze and would never buy a stand alone GPS ever again.  I downloaded it immediately.  In the beginning, I was a bit frustrated with Waze and how hard it kept working to take me on routes I did not want.  However, I started trusting it and have really seen a change! I like that it gives me alternate routes and shows me where the slow downs are. That makes for an informed decision as I make the trek to and from work.  I love that Waze has learned the routes I take most frequently and can help me navigate through and around them.  I also appreciate that it is generally up to date because of the social aspect.  The only cons I have are the frequency of the pop ups when I am in really slow traffic, occasional issues with loading maps, and that it does not automatically mute itself when I am on a call.  The issues with maps are generally solved by restarting the app, but this is not a great plan when you are trying to drive and be hands free.  Overall, Waze is a great product.  Enjoying it a lot!

review_text: This app is completely deserving of it. It's just not ready for prime-time.   1. I was traveling out of town and decided to give this a shot for the social aspect (read police reporting). That's about the best part of this application. In ~500 miles of windshield time, I encountered maybe 10 Wazers on the map. So the freshness wasn't there. No useful police reports, I did report my ~8 radar traps on both sides of the road encountered.   2. Maps/Routing. Not knowing where I was going since I was going to a new customer site, I used the navigation aspect of Waze. It led me on a very strange route according to the customer when I arrived. Two times it told me to turn on an overpass/underpass where there was no entrance to the highway, taking me several additional miles to find highway entry points. I submitted map modifications, but they don't show in the system.   3. Reporting, as I said, I drove ~500 miles, not even 200 miles show up on the console.   4. Crashes. Anytime you come out of the app for a data request, the app crashes. Likely the reason #3 was under reported.   5. Update interval. Lags behind a couple seconds, in tight proximity exits, you can (I did) miss a turn.
